danger_signals.visual_form: Animated catastrophe overlays (floods, tornadoes, fires, earthquakes, monster attacks)
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threats to city infrastructure and population; triggers economic/population decline

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual degradation of residential areas; reduced building quality
danger_signals.meaning: Low safety in district; residents avoid or downgrade housing; population dissatisfaction
Infrastructure breakdown; cascading service failures in affected area

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual contamination indicators near industrial zones and power plants
danger_signals.meaning: Reduced residential desirability; health impact on population
Movement impedance; reduces commercial/residential development attractiveness

danger_signals.visual_form: UI alerts; deteriorating building conditions citywide
danger_signals.meaning: Game-ending failure state; city collapse imminent

safety_signals.visual_form: Illuminated/powered buildings and infrastructure on map
safety_signals.meaning: Functional services; prerequisite for residential/commercial development

safety_signals.visual_form: High-quality residential building graphics; well-maintained appearance
safety_signals.meaning: Safe neighborhoods; attracts population and enables premium housing

safety_signals.visual_form: Distributed hospitals, fire stations, police stations across districts
safety_signals.meaning: Emergency response capability; reduces disaster/crime severity
Quality of life improvement; increases residential desirability

safety_signals.visual_form: Rising population density; upgraded building types appearing
safety_signals.meaning: Healthy city state; tax revenue increasing; population satisfaction high

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Zoned residential areas; density varies by economic tier
Commercial zones; typically cluster near traffic intersections; benefit from low crime
Industrial zones; generate pollution; create jobs but reduce nearby residential desirability

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Police station, Fire station, Hospital, Power plant graphics
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Distributed strategically; coverage radius determines service effectiveness; visual distinction from private structures

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Small scattered residential clusters; minimal infrastructure; mostly empty grid
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Sims slowly construct basic housing; limited commercial activity; low tax revenue

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rapid densification of zones; upgraded building types appearing; sprawling infrastructure; visible traffic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Population growth accelerates; Sims upgrade to better housing; commercial areas thrive; construction continues rapidly

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dense cityscape with mixed-use districts; established power grid; comprehensive transportation; parks and services distributed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Population stabilizes at high levels; steady tax revenue; Sims maintain rather than expand; service demands balanced

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Catastrophe animation overlay; destroyed buildings reset to empty lots; flames/water/debris visual effects; blackouts if power plant damaged
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Population flees affected zones; Sims stop construction; service collapse in impacted area; economic downturn

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Building downgrade (houses → abandoned lots); reduced population density; abandoned commercial structures; visible decay
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Sims downgrade housing or leave; tax revenue drops; businesses close; Sims avoid constructing in declining areas

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Visible environmental contamination around industrial zones and power plants; visual degradation of nearby residential areas
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Sims avoid building near pollution sources; residential zones downgrade; health impact; population migration away from contamination

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dense transportation network visualization; crowded road graphics; visible traffic flow impedance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Sims relocate away from congested areas; reduced development appeal; commercial performance declines in traffic zones

environmental_storytelling.example: Distinct building graphics for residential (houses/apartments), commercial (shops/offices), and industrial (factories/warehouses) telegraph the functional purpose and economic class of each district to the player
Power plants and electrical grids are visually distinct; unpowered zones appear darkened, immediately communicating to player that infrastructure must precede development
Police stations, fire stations, and hospitals are distinct visual elements; their placement and density communicate law enforcement, fire safety, and health service availability
Industrial zones visually contaminate adjacent residential areas, creating visual narrative of industrial externalities affecting quality of life
Residential zones display upgraded housing (multi-story apartments, better-maintained structures) as population density and wealth increase, showing prosperity through visual evolution
Animated tornado, earthquake, flood, and monster attacks provide dramatic visual communication of risk; destruction visible on map reinforces consequence of poor planning
Road networks and transportation systems are visually prominent; congestion appears as dense overlays, communicating the need for infrastructure expansion

environmental_storytelling.technique: Economic health indicators through building state
environmental_storytelling.example: Abandoned/degraded buildings signal economic decline; upgraded structures signal prosperity; visual building condition directly represents zone health

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Construct and upgrade housing; build apartment blocks and premium dwellings; population density increases
Downgrade housing; avoid construction; population declines; migrate away
Avoid building; downgrade existing structures; population migration away
Cease construction; do not upgrade housing; abandon developed areas
Build and expand commercial structures; cluster near intersections and residential areas
Avoid expanding; relocate away; performance declines
Build factories and warehouses; locate away from residential to minimize pollution complaints
Still build but create pollution externality; trigger residential downgrade and migration
Animate destruction; destroy buildings; kill population; cause economic collapse; trigger migration
Reduce crime, fire damage, health issues; enable residential upgrade; increase desirability
Sims construct; upgrade buildings; expand zones; increase density; tax revenue grows
Sims abandon buildings; downgrade structures; reduce density; migration away; tax revenue collapses

notes: SimCity (1989) uses a non-action simulation paradigm where world-state is communicated almost entirely through environmental visual design and Sim behavioral responses. There are no explicit factions in a political sense, but rather functional economic sectors (residential, commercial, industrial, municipal) that compete for resources and space. The game's core manifestation system is the building upgrade/downgrade cycle: as conditions improve, Sims build better structures; as conditions deteriorate, structures visually degrade. Disasters provide dramatic, explicit danger signaling through animated catastrophe overlays. Infrastructure (power grid, roads, services) are visually distinct and their presence/absence directly controls what Sims can and will build. The game teaches urban planning and systems thinking through visual feedback loops where player decisions manifest as world-state changes that immediately produce visual and behavioral consequences.
